 - Developer Ergonomics & Infrastructure Debt (multi‑PR initiative) - Unify package management (pnpm workspaces) - Create at repo root - Remove - Added a repository that pins Corepack to so every workspace install uses the expected package manager version - Remove obsolete npm configs & warnings - Delete and keys from - Document proxy environment variables in - Husky hardening for CI - Move to a postinstall script gated by presence - Add script that skips hook execution in CI - Automate - Add to pre‑approve native deps ( , , ) - Dependency upgrades & cleanup - Replace deprecated packages ( , ) - Remove in favor of native API - Upgrade ESLint to current LTS and align plugins - Introduce weekly workflow - Security & audit pipeline - Add GitHub Dependabot ( , weekly) - Create that blocks merges on high‑severity issues - Test harness improvements - Make run all suites by default - Emit a warning if zero tests are detected (unit & E2E) - Quest tooling enhancements - Script to generate UUIDs & inject item/process refs - Pre‑commit hook to validate quests before push - Hardening metadata refactor - Extract blocks to - Build‑time script to re‑inject metadata - Docs & build noise - Rename orphan folder to match actual path - Add Prism plaintext mapping to silence “language text” warnings - Removed the hardcoded numeric item ID from the shop index so the featured buy and sell chips lead to the correct item slug - Security automation - Ship a staged-diff secret scanner so PRs catch credential-like strings early - CI speed & log cleanliness - Silence non‑critical build messages - Consolidate install logs for readability - Cached the pnpm store in CI so each job reuses previously downloaded packages - Node version standardization - Add and document Node.js 20 LTS requirement
 Authentication for the quest submission form was audited. Tokens are now saved in IndexedDB so you don't need to re‑enter them, and you can clear them at any time. See the updated Authentication Flow documentation for details.

title: March 1, 2026
heading: 10x More Quests
text: Finished all the quests already? As of v2.1 and its handful of quests, you could 100% the game in a little over a month. And most of the time you'd just be waiting for some multi‑week progress bar to complete. It was like watching paint dry.

title: March 1, 2026
heading: Quest detail page
text:
- Quest detail views now hydrate directly from the canonical quest JSON, so you see the full branching dialogue, NPC art, and rewards for any built-in quest without relying on placeholder text.
 Thanks to the new custom quest system, I managed to make all the new quests in‑game without touching a single JSON file directly. Well, except when it came time to merge v3 into the main branch. Speaking of... Work has begun on several new quest trees, including Chemistry, Astronomy, and Programming. Early stubs are now in place, paving the way toward the promised quest expansion.
 Astronomy now includes a meteor shower quest, bringing us closer to the full expansion.
 As of this release, DSPACE includes 246 official quests. For reference, v2.1 contained only 22 quests. Here's how the totals compare:
 | Release | Quest Count | | --------- | ----------- | | v2.1 | 22 | | v3 (HEAD) | 246 |
 With 246 quests, we've achieved the 10x milestone and then some—that's 11.2x more content than v2.1! The new custom quest system made adding content much faster.

title: March 1, 2026
heading: Custom Quests
text:
You can now create your own custom quests on‑device for whatever you want! The game's source code contains the set of all official quests (which generally in some way relate to space exploration, however loosely), and anything on your device is considered non‑canon. However, if you choose to contribute to the base game, there's also a procedure to make your quests canon by submitting a pull request. The in‑game UI helps you do this without touching a single line of code or opening a text editor. Just follow the Quest Submission Guide.
 Quest art now stays on your machine, too—the quest editor converts uploaded images into data URLs, keeping previews and saved custom quests completely local even before you share them.

title: March 1, 2026
heading: Custom Items & Processes
text:
Not only can you create custom quests, but v3 also introduces the ability to create your own items and processes! Want to design a new type of rocket fuel? Create a custom manufacturing process? Now you can! The game includes:
 - A visual editor for creating new items at /items/create - A process designer for creating custom manufacturing chains at /processes/create - Built‑in validation to ensure your creations will work - The ability to preview and test before finalizing - NEW: Unified bundle submission workflow at /bundles/submit that keeps related quests, items, and processes together in a single pull request
 Like custom quests, your creations are stored locally by default. When you're ready to contribute, package everything into a bundle following the Custom Content Bundles format and submit it through the in-game interface. This ensures related content stays synchronized and makes reviews more efficient.

title: March 1, 2026
heading: Process System Improvements
text: - Material and machine requirements are now enforced at process start, closing the gap first flagged in the October 19, 2022 release notes.

title: March 1, 2026
heading: UI Polish
text: - Hazard cards now share the same accessible markup as active cards while rendering with a distinct warning background, making dangerous actions easier to spot at a glance. - Added a lint-backed accessibility check that requires every interactive button to declare an explicit , preventing accidental form submissions when navigating by keyboard. - Enhanced accessibility linting to validate attributes, catching empty or whitespace-only labels that fail to provide meaningful context for assistive technologies.

title: March 1, 2026
heading: Streamlined Game Systems
text:
We've simplified DSPACE's core mechanics to focus purely on what matters most: the joy of space exploration and the real‑world impact of your achievements. The game now operates with a cleaner, more direct connection between virtual progress and real‑world space advancement.
 Our virtual units (dWatt, dUSD, etc.) remain as intuitive measurements of your progress, but they're now more tightly integrated with tangible outcomes. This change emerged from community feedback and our commitment to maintaining a clear path between in‑game achievements and real‑world space development.

title: March 1, 2026
heading: Local‑First Architecture
text:
We've rebuilt DSPACE's architecture to be truly local‑first. Your progress, achievements, and contributions are now stored primarily on your own device. This approach gives you complete control over your data.
 A few technical details that might interest you:
 - Your v2 progress will automatically migrate to v3. On first launch, v3 checks for the legacy key in . If found, it copies the data into IndexedDB and removes the old keys. - All custom content includes rollback functionality (in case something goes wrong)
 The new Cloud Sync feature lets you back up your progress across devices using a private GitHub gist. Visit the Cloud Sync page to enable it.

title: March 1, 2026
heading: Refined Virtual Economy
text:
We've removed all blockchain integration plans from DSPACE. While Web3 features were originally planned, we realized they could potentially harm our core mission of democratizing space exploration. The virtual units (dWatt, dUSD, etc.) work perfectly well as simple progress metrics without needing tokenization.
 Coming in future updates: For social features and cross‑instance communication, we're planning to implement ActivityPub. This will give us all the benefits of decentralized social networking without the downsides of blockchain integration. Your achievements, collaborations, and contributions will be shareable and verifiable across instances, just in a simpler and more reliable way.

title: March 1, 2026
heading: AI Integration Updates
text: Instead of relying on commercial LLM providers like OpenAI, DSPACE now uses our own distributed computing project token.place. This allows us to use idle resources at home (and other people's homes, eventually) to fulfill the LLM needs of non‑business‑critical applications such as DSPACE. Both DSPACE and token.place are intended to be fully runnable on cheap home hardware, and are therefore both fully open source and permissively licensed.

title: March 1, 2026
heading: dChat knowledge upgrade
text: dChat now ships with an in-game knowledge base that summarizes canonical quests, items, processes, and the player's local inventory snapshot. This gives the assistant immediate context to answer gameplay questions without waiting for future updates or external documentation lookups.

title: March 1, 2026
heading: Community Features
text: Coming in future updates: Enhanced social features focused on direct collaboration between players. You'll be able to share your achievements, help others with their space missions, and contribute to collective goals – all without any intermediary systems. This will create a more immediate and meaningful connection between players working toward common space exploration objectives.

title: March 1, 2026
heading: Self‑hosted
text: Starting with DSPACE 2.1, the website is self‑hosted on premises, instead of deploying via Netlify. It's currently running on an M4 Mac Mini. Redundancy is here.

title: March 1, 2026
heading: Switching to MIT License
text: While 0BSD sounds like a nice license, it is in some cases not on the list of accepted OSS licenses on the Internet. Therefore, I'm switching from 0BSD to MIT, which is still very permissive but much better supported.

title: March 1, 2026
heading: Looking Forward
text:
These changes reflect our commitment to keeping DSPACE focused on its core mission: democratizing space exploration through education, simulation, and real‑world impact. By removing unnecessary complexity, we've made the game more accessible while strengthening the connection between virtual achievements and actual space development.
 Thank you for being part of this journey. The future of space exploration belongs to everyone, and DSPACE will continue evolving to make that future more accessible to all.
